message:
test(vdbe): add IN-table-name and GROUP BY alias star expansion test coverage

Adds tests for SQL features that were recently implemented but lacked
dedicated coverage:

- WHERE a IN t2 / WHERE a NOT IN t2: validates table-name IN probe for
  SELECT queries (the existing bd-3qlh work enabled runtime support).
- UPDATE ... WHERE a IN t2: verifies IN-table probe works for UPDATE DML.
- DELETE ... WHERE a IN t2: verifies IN-table probe works for DELETE DML.
- SELECT t.* FROM ga AS t GROUP BY t.k: validates that table-aliased star
  expansion works correctly when combined with GROUP BY (bd-2wiu).

Beads: synced issue tracker state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2oav","title":"WAL-FEC: Pipelined background FEC job (durable vs repairable semantics)","description":"Implement the default required pipelining for repair symbol generation.

Spec rules (normative):
- Commit is durable once .wal frames are written+fsync and wal-index updated.
- Commit becomes repairable only after WalFecGroupMeta + R SymbolRecords are appended to .wal-fec and fsync.
- Encoding MUST NOT happen inside WAL write critical section.

Implementation:
- After commit durability, enqueue background job:
  - read source frames from .wal
  - deterministically generate repair symbols
  - append meta+symbols to .wal-fec
  - fsync .wal-fec

Optional:
- Synchronous mode MAY wait for .wal-fec fsync before ack commit; off by default.

Done when:
- concurrency/ordering is correct and tested.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:38:12.731973216Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:14.685933956Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T21:50:09.033701027Z","close_reason":"Pipelined background FEC job implemented via WalFecRepairPipeline in wal_fec.rs.  {"id":"bd-2pcg","title":"Overhead: Implement PRAGMA raptorq_overhead (defaults, persistence, per-object policy)","description":"Implement `PRAGMA raptorq_overhead` as specified.

## Semantics
- `PRAGMA raptorq_overhead = <percent>` sets the default multiplicative overhead policy.
- The value is used by budgeting to derive `R` from `K_source`:
  - `R = max(slack_decode, ceil(K_source * overhead_percent / 100))` with `slack_decode=2`.

## Persistence / Scope
- Define whether the setting is:
  - connection-local (default SQLite style), and/or
  - persisted into database metadata for replication determinism.

If persisted, it MUST be versioned and included in any object metadata required for deterministic decoding/proofs.

## Interaction With Object Types
- Provide a mechanism to override overhead per object class (markers vs pages vs index segments), even if V1 exposes only a global PRAGMA.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Setting is parsed/validated (bounds, rounding rules) and stored deterministically.
- Budgeting API exposes the effective overhead used for each object (for evidence/proofs).
- Unit tests cover:
  - bounds clamping
  - rounding behavior
  - small-K behavior (no under-provisioning)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:53:34.855360204Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:08:50.790213962Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T22:08:50.790188815Z","close_reason":"Implemented PRAGMA raptorq_overhead policy state: deterministic parse/clamp/rounding, optional versioned persisted metadata scope, per-object overrides, and policy_controller unit coverage for bounds/rounding/small-K/exposure.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2pcg","depends_on_id":"bd-3lpo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:54:38.549657004Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:21:39Z"},{"id":1122,"issue_id":"bd-2r4z","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Build FEC sidecar generation utility (db-fec and wal-fec)

### What
Build the utility that generates RaptorQ erasure-coded sidecar files (.db-fec, .wal-fec) for a given database. These sidecars contain the repair symbols that enable corruption recovery.

### Background (§3.4.6 Erasure-Coded Page Storage)
- Database divided into page groups of G=64 pages (256KB)
- Each group encoded with R=4 repair symbols
- Repair symbols stored in `.db-fec` sidecar file
- Sidecar header includes `db_gen_digest` = BLAKE3(change_counter + page_count + freelist_count + schema_cookie)
- Per-source validation via xxh3_128 hash

### Design
```rust
pub struct FecGenerator {
    page_size: usize,
    group_size: usize,   // G = 64 (default)
    repair_count: usize, // R = 4 (default)
}

pub struct FecSidecar {
    pub db_gen_digest: [u8; 32],  // BLAKE3 of database identity
    pub groups: Vec<PageGroup>,
}

pub struct PageGroup {
    pub group_index: u32,
    pub source_pages: Vec<u32>,     // Page numbers in this group
    pub repair_symbols: Vec<Vec<u8>>,  // R repair symbols, each page_size bytes
    pub source_hashes: Vec<[u8; 16]>,  // xxh3_128 per source page
}

impl FecGenerator {
    /// Generate .db-fec sidecar for a database file
    pub fn generate_db_fec(&self, db_path: &Path) -> Result<FecSidecar> { ... }

    /// Generate .wal-fec sidecar for a WAL file
    pub fn generate_wal_fec(&self, wal_path: &Path) -> Result<WalFecSidecar> { ... }

    /// Attempt to repair a corrupted page using its group's FEC data
    pub fn repair_page(&self, sidecar: &FecSidecar, db_path: &Path, page_number: u32) -> Result<Vec<u8>> { ... }
}
```

### RaptorQ Integration
Uses the `fsqlite-raptorq` crate (already in workspace) for:
- `encode()`: Generate repair symbols from source pages
- `decode()`: Reconstruct corrupted pages from surviving sources + repairs
- `verify()`: Validate per-source xxh3_128 hashes

### Header Page Special Case (§3.4.6)
Page 1 (header) uses G=1, R=4 (400% redundancy). The header is so critical that losing it makes the entire database unreadable, so we store 4 repair copies.

### db_gen_digest Calculation
```rust
fn compute_db_gen_digest(db_path: &Path) -> [u8; 32] {
    let header = read_first_100_bytes(db_path);
    let change_counter = u32::from_be_bytes(header[24..28]);
    let page_count = u32::from_be_bytes(header[28..32]);
    let freelist_count = u32::from_be_bytes(header[36..40]);
    let schema_cookie = u32::from_be_bytes(header[40..44]);
    blake3::hash(&[change_counter, page_count, freelist_count, schema_cookie].concat_bytes())
}
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- .db-fec generation works for any SQLite database
- Repair successfully recovers a corrupted page when R > corrupted pages in group
- Per-source xxh3_128 validation detects corruption
- db_gen_digest rejects stale sidecars
- Header page has G=1, R=4 special handling
- Unit tests for encode → corrupt → decode cycle

### Issue: Dependency on fsqlite-raptorq API surface
This bead assumes fsqlite-raptorq provides `encode()` and `decode()` functions for RaptorQ encoding. Before implementing, verify:
1. `crates/fsqlite-raptorq/` exists and has these APIs
2. The API accepts page-sized chunks as source symbols
3. The API returns repair symbols of the same size
4. The decode function handles the "K symbols needed, K+R available" math

If the API doesn't match, either:
- Adapt the FEC generator to the existing API
- Or extend the raptorq crate with the needed functions (separate bead)

### Testing: Full encode→corrupt→decode roundtrip
The key test is the full cycle:
```rust
#[test]
fn test_fec_roundtrip() {
    let db = create_test_database(1000);  // 1000 rows, multiple pages
    let sidecar = FecGenerator::default().generate_db_fec(&db)?;

    // Corrupt one page
    let injector = CorruptionInjector::new(db.clone());
    injector.inject(&CorruptionPattern::PageZero { page_number: 5 })?;

    // Repair
    let repaired = FecGenerator::default().repair_page(&sidecar, &db, 5)?;
    let original = read_page(&golden_db, 5)?;
    assert_eq!(repaired, original, "Page not correctly repaired");
}
```

### Implementation (crates/fsqlite-core/src/db_fec.rs)

Added the following public API to the existing db_fec module:

**Data structures:**
- `DbHeaderFields` — parsed SQLite header fields for FEC generation

**Generation functions:**
- `parse_db_header_fields(data)` — parse header from raw bytes
- `read_db_header_fields(path)` — parse header from file
- `generate_db_fec_from_bytes(db_data)` — generate complete .db-fec sidecar from raw database bytes
- `generate_db_fec_sidecar(db_path)` — generate sidecar from file path
- `write_db_fec_sidecar(db_path)` — generate and write .db-fec sidecar to disk

**Read/repair functions:**
- `read_db_fec_header(sidecar_path)` — read DbFecHeader from .db-fec file
- `read_db_fec_group_for_page(sidecar_data, header, target_pgno)` — read group meta + repair symbols for a page
- `db_fec_path_for_db(db_path)` — compute .db-fec sidecar path

### Sidecar Layout
`[DbFecHeader (52B)][Seg_page1][Seg_group0][Seg_group1]...`
- General segments padded to full_segment_len for O(1) random access
- Page 1: G=1, R=4 (400% redundancy)
- Other pages: G=64, R=4

### R*-Tree (§14.4, `fsqlite-ext-rtree`)
Efficient spatial indexing (Beckmann et al., SIGMOD 1990). SQLite uses R*-tree variant (not original Guttman 1984).

`CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE idx USING rtree(id, minX, maxX, minY, maxY)`. 1-5 dimensions (2-10 coordinate columns). 32-bit floats default; `rtree_i32` for integers.

**Queries:** Range queries (bounding box constraints). Custom geometry callbacks implementing `RtreeGeometry: Send+Sync` with `query_func(bbox) → Include|Exclude|PartiallyContained`. Tree descent prunes Exclude branches.

**Geopoly extension:** Built on R*-tree. geopoly_overlap, within, area, blob, json, svg, bbox, contains_point, group_bbox, regular, ccw, xform. Polygons: 4-byte header + pairs of f32 coordinates.

### Session (§14.5, `fsqlite-ext-session`)
Records changes as changesets/patchsets for cross-database application.

**Changeset format:** Per table: 'T' byte, column count, PK flags, table name. Per row: operation byte (INSERT=18, DELETE=9, UPDATE=23). DELETE: old values. INSERT: new values. UPDATE: old+new values (undefined for unchanged non-PK). Values: type byte (0=undefined, 1=integer, 2=real, 3=text, 4=blob, 5=null) + data (varint-length-prefixed for text/blob, 8B BE for int/real).

**Conflict resolution:** ConflictAction enum (OmitChange, Replace, Abort). ConflictType enum (Data, NotFound, Conflict, Constraint, ForeignKey).

**Patchsets:** More compact: omits old values for UPDATE (only new+PK). Cannot detect conflicts as precisely.

### ICU (§14.6, `fsqlite-ext-icu`)
Unicode-aware string operations.

**Collation:** `icu_load_collation('de_DE', 'german')` creates collation from ICU locale. Uses `ucol_strcoll`.
**Case folding:** icu_upper/icu_lower(X, LOCALE) — locale-aware (vs ASCII-only built-in).
**FTS tokenizer:** `tokenize='icu zh_CN'` for language-aware word breaking via `UBreakIterator`. Critical for CJK.

### Miscellaneous (§14.7, `fsqlite-ext-misc`)
**generate_series(START,STOP [,STEP]):** Virtual table. Columns: value, start, stop, step.
**dbstat:** B-tree page usage stats (name, path, pageno, pagetype, ncell, payload, unused, mx_payload). aggregate hidden column.
**dbpage:** Direct page read/write. `SELECT data FROM dbpage WHERE pgno=1`.
**csv:** Virtual table for CSV files. filename, header, columns options.
**decimal:** Arbitrary-precision. decimal(), decimal_add/sub/mul, decimal_sum, decimal_cmp. String representation avoids float precision loss.
**uuid:** uuid() v4, uuid_str(X) blob→string, uuid_blob(X) string→16-byte blob.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T05:16:43Z"},{"id":103,"issue_id":"bd-2rcq","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §14.5.1-§14.5.3 Full Spec Extract + Test/Logging Requirements (Session Extension)

This comment exists because the earlier bead summary for Session covered the concepts but did not explicitly include the numbered sub-subsections §14.5.1..§14.5.3.

### Session (`fsqlite-ext-session`)

The Session extension records changes to a database and represents them
as changesets or patchsets that can be applied to other databases.

#### §14.5.1 Changeset Format

A changeset is a binary blob with the following layout:
```
For each modified table:
  'T' byte (0x54)
  Number of columns (varint)
  For each column: 0x00 (not part of PK) or 0x01 (part of PK)
  Table name (nul-terminated string)

  For each changed row:
    Operation byte: SQLITE_INSERT (18), SQLITE_DELETE (9), SQLITE_UPDATE (23)

    For DELETE:
      Old values: one value per column (serial-type encoded)

    For INSERT:
      New values: one value per column (serial-type encoded)

    For UPDATE:
      Old values: one per column (undefined for non-PK columns that didn't change)
      New values: one per column (undefined for columns that didn't change)
```

Each value is encoded as: a single type byte (0x00=undefined, 0x01=integer,
0x02=real, 0x03=text, 0x04=blob, 0x05=null) followed by the value data
(varint-length-prefixed for text and blob, 8-byte big-endian for integer
and real).

#### §14.5.2 Conflict Resolution

When applying a changeset, conflicts are resolved via a callback:
```rust
pub enum ConflictAction {
    OmitChange,     // skip this change
    Replace,        // overwrite conflicting row
    Abort,          // abort the entire apply operation
}

pub enum ConflictType {
    Data,           // row exists but values differ from expected
    NotFound,       // row to update/delete does not exist
    Conflict,       // unique constraint violation
    Constraint,     // other constraint violation
    ForeignKey,     // foreign key constraint
}
```

#### §14.5.3 Patchset Differences

A patchset is a more compact format that omits the old values for UPDATE
operations (only stores new values and PK). Patchsets cannot detect
conflicts as precisely as changesets (cannot verify that the old row matched)
but are significantly smaller for tables with many columns.

### Additional FrankenSQLite Requirements (Bead-local)

1. Unit tests MUST cover:
   - changeset encode/decode round-trip for INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE
   - correct handling of undefined old/new values in UPDATE rows
   - value encoding for each type byte, including NULL and BLOB lengths
   - conflict callback invocation for each ConflictType, with each ConflictAction
2. E2E tests MUST cover:
   - generate changeset from DB-A, apply to DB-B, then verify DB-A == DB-B for the affected tables
   - negative tests: apply changeset where DB-B has diverged to force Data/NotFound/Conflict/Constraint/ForeignKey
   - patchset vs changeset behavioral difference: changeset detects mismatch where patchset cannot
3. Logging MUST include:
   - per apply: table name, op counts by kind, and whether conflicts occurred
   - on conflict: ConflictType, chosen ConflictAction, and a compact diff of expected vs found values

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:20:09Z"},{"id":1067,"issue_id":"bd-6d9v","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: Benchmark — large transaction (100K/1M row single-txn insert)

### What
Measure performance of very large transactions that stress the WAL and page cache.

### Benchmark Design
- Single transaction: BEGIN, INSERT N rows, COMMIT
- N = 100,000 and 1,000,000
- Table with 5 columns: id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT, email TEXT, score REAL, created TEXT
- Row size ~200 bytes → 100K rows ≈ 20MB, 1M rows ≈ 200MB

### Why This Matters
Large transactions exercise:
- WAL growth (C SQLite: WAL file grows until checkpoint)
- MVCC version chain (FrankenSQLite: page versions accumulate)
- Memory pressure (page cache turnover)
- Commit latency (fsync of large WAL vs MVCC commit)

### Metrics
- Total time for INSERT phase
- Commit latency (time from last INSERT to COMMIT completion)
- Peak memory usage during transaction
- WAL file size at commit time (C SQLite) / version chain depth (FrankenSQLite)
- Post-VACUUM database size (should be identical)

### Expected Behavior
- FrankenSQLite may have advantages in commit speed (no WAL checkpoint needed)
- C SQLite may have advantages in insert speed (mature, optimized C code)
- Memory characteristics will differ due to MVCC vs WAL approach

### Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks for both 100K and 1M row transactions
- Total time, commit latency, and memory reported
- Both backends produce same final state (logical comparison)
- No OOM or crash on 1M row transaction

 {"id":"bd-zcdn","title":"§5.6.5 GC Coordination: Horizon, Scheduling, Incremental Pruning","description":"## §5.6.5 GC Coordination: Horizon, Scheduling, Incremental Pruning
Spec location: Lines 8012-8147 of COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md

### Overview
The garbage collection coordination subsystem for MVCC version chains. This is critical for preventing unbounded memory growth. Covers gc_horizon computation, GC scheduling policy, raise_gc_horizon() algorithm, and coordination with sentinel states (CLAIMING/CLEANING).

### gc_horizon (SharedMemoryLayout)
- gc_horizon is a monotonically increasing CommitSeq safe-point: `min(begin_seq)` across all active transactions
- Since begin_seq derives from monotonically increasing published commit_seq, gc_horizon never decreases
- gc_horizon is authoritative ONLY when advanced by the commit sequencer (other processes read-only)

### GC Scheduling Policy (Alien-Artifact Discipline)
```
f_gc = min(f_max, max(f_min, version_chain_pressure / target_chain_length))
```
- f_max = 100 Hz (never GC more often than every 10ms)
- f_min = 1 Hz (always GC at least once per second)
- version_chain_pressure = observed mean version chain length (BOCPD-tracked)
- target_chain_length = 8 (from Theorem 5: R*D+1 for R=100, D=0.07s)
- WHO runs GC: commit coordinator runs raise_gc_horizon() after each group commit batch
- Only the process holding WAL write lock (coordinator) runs GC — avoids thundering herd
- Other processes observe updated gc_horizon on their next read

### raise_gc_horizon() Algorithm (Normative Pseudocode)
```
raise_gc_horizon():
    old_horizon = shm.gc_horizon.load(Acquire)
    global_min_begin_seq = shm.commit_seq.load(Acquire)
    for slot in txn_slots:
        tid = slot.txn_id.load(Acquire)
        if tid == 0: continue
        if decode_tag(tid) != 0:
            // CRITICAL: Sentinel-tagged slots (CLAIMING/CLEANING) are horizon blockers.
            // A claiming slot may have captured snapshot but not published real txn_id.
            global_min_begin_seq = min(global_min_begin_seq, old_horizon)
            continue
        global_min_begin_seq = min(global_min_begin_seq, slot.begin_seq.load(Acquire))
    new_horizon = max(old_horizon, global_min_begin_seq)  // monotonic
    shm.gc_horizon.store(new_horizon, Release)
```

### Normative Rules
1. gc_horizon MUST be monotonically non-decreasing (new_horizon = max(old, computed))
2. CLAIMING/CLEANING sentinel-tagged TxnSlots MUST block horizon advance
3. GC frequency formula MUST use the specified constants (f_max=100Hz, f_min=1Hz, target=8)
4. Only the commit coordinator (WAL write lock holder) runs GC
5. gc_horizon uses Acquire/Release memory ordering for cross-process visibility

### Error Conditions
- No active transactions: safe point = latest commit_seq (all versions beyond gc reclaimable)
- Sentinel-tagged slot race: horizon pinned to old_horizon to prevent premature version reclamation
- Coordinator crash: gc_horizon persists in SHM; new coordinator reads with Acquire ordering

### Logging Requirements
- DEBUG: GC horizon computation: oldest_active_begin_seq, horizon, blocked_by
- INFO: pruning batch summary: pages_pruned, versions_dropped, duration_ms
- WARN: GC lag beyond budget with suggested mitigation

### Child Bead
- §5.6.5.1 In-Process Version Pruning (bd-3t3.10): The actual memory reclamation implementation

### Dependencies
- Depends on: §5.6.1 SharedMemoryLayout (bd-3t3.5) for SHM region
- Depends on: §5.6.2 TxnSlot (bd-3t3.6) for slot scanning and sentinel states
- Related to: §6.5-6.7 ARC cache (ghost lists, eviction rules)

Crate: fsqlite-mvcc

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] GC horizon is correctly computed as the minimum active read timestamp across all live transactions
- [ ] Incremental pruning reclaims obsolete page versions without blocking concurrent readers
- [ ] GC scheduling avoids starvation under high write load while maintaining bounded memory growth
- [ ] SHM region memory layout (bd-3t3.5) is correctly used for slot scanning and sentinel states
- [ ] No version visible to any active transaction is ever reclaimed (safety invariant)

### What This Implements
The garbage collection coordination subsystem for MVCC version chains. This is critical for preventing unbounded memory growth.

### Spec Content (Lines 8012-8147)

**gc_horizon** in shared memory is a monotonically increasing safe-point in CommitSeq space: `min(begin_seq)` across all active transactions. Only advanced by the commit sequencer. Other processes treat it as read-only.

**GC Scheduling Policy (quantitative, not "periodically"):**
```
f_gc = min(f_max, max(f_min, version_chain_pressure / target_chain_length))
```
- f_max = 100 Hz (cap: never GC more often than every 10ms)
- f_min = 1 Hz (floor: always GC at least once per second)
- version_chain_pressure = observed mean version chain length (BOCPD-tracked)
- target_chain_length = 8 (from Theorem 5: R*D+1 for R=100, D=0.07s)

**Who runs GC:** Commit coordinator after each group commit batch (piggy-backing on commit critical section). Only the coordinator process runs GC. Other processes observe the updated gc_horizon on next read.

**raise_gc_horizon() pseudocode (normative):**
- Default: if no active txns exist, safe point is latest commit_seq
- Scan TxnSlots: CRITICAL — treat CLAIMING/CLEANING sentinel-tagged slots as horizon blockers
- new_horizon = max(old_horizon, global_min_begin_seq) — monotonic guarantee

### §5.6.5.1 In-Process Version Pruning (Required)
Advancing gc_horizon defines which versions are reclaimable (Theorem 4) but doesn't reclaim memory. MUST implement incremental, touched-page-driven pruning with strict work budgets:
- GcTodo queue: VecDeque<PageNumber> + HashSet for dedup
- on_publish_or_materialize_version(pgno) → enqueue
- gc_tick(): pop pages, prune chains under VersionArena write guard
- Work budgets (normative): pages_budget=64, versions_budget=4096
- prune_page_chain walks from head, finds oldest version <= horizon, frees everything older

**ARC interaction:** Removed versions must be eligible for eviction from ARC indexes and ghost lists.
**I/O boundary:** prune_page_chain is pure in-memory work. MUST NOT perform file reads.

2. test_gc_sentinel_blocking: CLAIMING/CLEANING slots block horizon advance
3. test_gc_scheduling_frequency: f_gc formula correctness at min/max/normal

6. test_arc_eviction_on_prune: ARC ghost lists cleaned after version pruning
7. test_no_io_during_prune: Verify prune_page_chain never issues file reads

### E2E Test
Spawn N writers, M readers. Run for duration D. Verify:
- Memory usage stays bounded (version chains don't grow unboundedly)
- No version visible to any active snapshot is pruned
- GC frequency adapts to write pressure

PURPOSE: Implement the gc_horizon computation and incremental version chain pruning.

## gc_horizon (SharedMemoryLayout)
- gc_horizon is a monotonically increasing CommitSeq safe-point: min(begin_seq) across all active txns
- Since begin_seq derives from monotonically increasing published commit_seq, gc_horizon never decreases
- gc_horizon is authoritative ONLY when advanced by the commit sequencer (other processes read-only)

## GC Scheduling Policy (Alien-Artifact)
- f_gc = min(f_max, max(f_min, version_chain_pressure / target_chain_length))
- f_max = 100 Hz (never GC more often than 10ms)
- f_min = 1 Hz (always GC at least once per second)
- version_chain_pressure = observed mean chain length (BOCPD-tracked)
- target_chain_length = 8 (from Theorem 5: R*D+1 for R=100, D=0.07s)
- WHO runs GC: commit coordinator runs raise_gc_horizon() after each group commit batch
- Only the process holding WAL write lock (coordinator) runs GC -- avoids thundering herd
- Other processes observe updated gc_horizon on their next read

## raise_gc_horizon() Algorithm (normative)
- Default: if no active txns, safe point = latest commit_seq
- Scan all TxnSlots:
  - Skip tid==0 (empty)
  - CRITICAL: Sentinel-tagged slots (CLAIMING/CLEANING) are horizon blockers
    - Use min(global_min_begin_seq, old_horizon) for sentinel slots
    - Reason: claiming slot may have captured snapshot but not published real txn_id yet
  - For real TxnId slots: min with slot.begin_seq
- new_horizon = max(old_horizon, global_min_begin_seq) -- monotonic
- Store with Release ordering

## In-Process Version Pruning (§5.6.5.1, REQUIRED)
- Advancing gc_horizon defines reclaimable versions (Theorem 4) but doesn't reclaim memory
- MUST implement incremental, touched-page-driven pruning with strict work budgets
- FORBIDDEN: naive scan-everything-under-VersionArena-write-guard (stop-the-world pauses)

### GcTodo Queue
- GcTodo { queue: VecDeque<PageNumber>, in_queue: HashSet<PageNumber> }
- on_publish_or_materialize_version(pgno): enqueue if not already present
- gc_tick(): pop pages from queue and prune their version chains

### Work Budgets (normative)
- pages_budget = 64
- versions_budget = 4096
- Lock VersionArena.write() only during actual pruning work

### prune_page_chain(pgno, horizon) Algorithm
- Walk chain from head down through versions newer than horizon
- Find committed version <= horizon -> becomes new tail
- Everything older is reclaimable by Theorem 4
- Sever chain: arena[cur].prev_idx = None
- Free all nodes beyond the severed point to free list

### ARC Interaction (normative)
- When committed version removed from chain, its cache entry MUST be eviction-eligible
- Remove (pgno, commit_seq) from ARC indexes and ghost lists (§6.7 coalescing + §6.6 durability)

### I/O Boundary (normative)
- prune_page_chain is pure in-memory work, MUST NOT perform file reads
- If pruned version later needed by old snapshot, resolve() consults durable store (§5.2, §7.11)

 {"id":"bd-zj56","title":"§5.10.2.1 Index Regeneration on Rebase: Partial Indexes + UNIQUE Enforcement","description":"Implement the 7-step index regeneration algorithm executed during deterministic rebase, handling partial indexes, expression indexes, UNIQUE enforcement, and overflow pages (spec lines 10240-10272).

SCOPE: During rebase, IndexDelete/IndexInsert ops associated with an UpdateExpression carry stale key bytes from the original snapshot. These MUST be discarded and regenerated from the schema and rebased row images. The rebase engine has access to the full schema at rebase time.

ALGORITHMS:
- 7-step Index Regeneration:
  (1) Discard stale index ops matching (table, rowid) of the rebased UpdateExpression
  (2) Enumerate secondary indexes from schema (ordinary, expression, UNIQUE, partial; MAY skip if provably independent of updated columns)
  (3) Compute participation for base and updated rows: ordinary indexes always true; partial indexes evaluate WHERE predicate against row
  (4) Compute index key bytes using SQLite affinity + collation rules matching VDBE/B-tree encoding
  (5) Emit index ops based on participation delta: base-only -> IndexDelete; updated-only -> IndexInsert; both with key change -> Delete+Insert; both with same key -> no-op
  (6) UNIQUE constraint re-validation: each IndexInsert enforces uniqueness against the new committed base snapshot; violation aborts rebase with SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE (true conflict, no silent merge)
  (7) Overflow and index chain mutation: handle overflow pages correctly for both IndexDelete (free chains) and IndexInsert (allocate chains)

INVARIANTS:
- Affinity coercion MUST use schema's declared column affinities
- Collation sequences MUST match index declarations
- Expression evaluation for expression indexes MUST be deterministic
- Skip optimization (Step 2) MUST be conservative: if in doubt, regenerate
- UNIQUE violations during rebase are true conflicts and MUST abort, not merge

TEST REQUIREMENTS (8 unit + 1 E2E):
- test_index_regen_ordinary_index_key_change, test_index_regen_partial_index_participation_change, test_index_regen_partial_index_entry_to_entry, test_index_regen_unique_constraint_violation_aborts, test_index_regen_expression_index, test_index_regen_no_op_when_key_unchanged, test_index_regen_multiple_indexes_same_table, test_index_regen_overflow_key_handling
- E2E: test_e2e_index_regeneration_on_rebase_partial_unique (concurrent writes with UNIQUE partial index, verify C sqlite3 behavior match)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
1. Stale index ops from original intent log correctly discarded for rebased rows
2. Partial index participation evaluated correctly for both base and updated rows
3. UNIQUE constraint violations abort with SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE
4. Expression indexes produce correct keys matching VDBE encoding
5. Overflow page chains correctly freed/allocated during index ops

**Spec reference:** COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md, lines 10240–10272

## Overview

During rebase, any `IndexDelete`/`IndexInsert` ops from the original intent log that are
associated with an `UpdateExpression` (same table, same rowid) carry **stale key bytes**
derived from the original snapshot. These MUST be discarded. The rebase engine MUST
regenerate index operations from the schema and the rebased row images using the 7-step
algorithm below.

## 7-Step Index Regeneration Algorithm

### Step 1: Discard stale index ops
All `IndexDelete`/`IndexInsert` ops in the original intent log that match the same
(table, rowid) as the UpdateExpression being rebased MUST be dropped entirely.

### Step 2: Enumerate secondary indexes from schema
Enumerate the table's secondary indexes from the schema, including:
- Ordinary indexes
- Expression indexes
- UNIQUE indexes
- Partial indexes (indexes with WHERE predicates)

The engine MAY skip an index **only** if it can prove the index's key and partial
predicate are independent of the updated columns.

### Step 3: Compute participation for base row and updated row
For each index, determine whether the **base** row (before update) and the **updated**
row (after update) participate in the index:
- **Ordinary/expression indexes:** participation is always `true`.
- **Partial indexes:** evaluate the index WHERE predicate against the row;
  participation is `true` iff the predicate evaluates to `true` (SQLite semantics).

### Step 4: Compute index key bytes
If participation is true, compute the index key bytes by evaluating the index key
definition against the row:
- **Ordinary index:** use the indexed column values.
- **Expression index:** evaluate the index expressions.

Key construction MUST apply **SQLite affinity + collation rules** for that index,
and MUST match the normal VDBE/B-tree index encoding.

### Step 5: Emit index ops based on participation delta
- If base participates and updated does NOT → emit `IndexDelete(index, old_key, rowid)`.
- If base does NOT participate and updated does → emit `IndexInsert(index, new_key, rowid)`.
- If both participate:
  - If `old_key != new_key` → emit `IndexDelete(old_key)` then `IndexInsert(new_key)`.
  - If `old_key == new_key` → no-op.

### Step 6: UNIQUE constraint re-validation (normative)
For UNIQUE indexes, each `IndexInsert` MUST enforce uniqueness against the **new committed
base snapshot**. If a conflicting key exists for a different rowid, rebase MUST abort with
the appropriate constraint error (true conflict). The engine MUST NOT "merge" the violation
or silently skip it.

### Step 7: Overflow and index chain mutation
Index key bytes may overflow a single B-tree cell. The engine MUST handle overflow pages
correctly during both `IndexDelete` (freeing overflow chains) and `IndexInsert` (allocating
new overflow chains). The engine uses the schema (needed for affinity coercion) and MUST use
it to enumerate indexes and evaluate index predicates/expressions deterministically.

## Implementation Requirements

- The rebase engine has access to the full schema at rebase time.
- Affinity coercion MUST use the schema's declared column affinities.
- Collation sequences MUST match those declared on the index.
- Expression evaluation for expression indexes MUST be deterministic.
- The skip optimization (Step 2) MUST be conservative: if in doubt, regenerate.

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: `test_index_regen_ordinary_index_key_change`
Rebase an UPDATE that changes an indexed column. Verify the old index key is deleted
and the new index key is inserted. Confirm the generated ops match what a fresh
VDBE encode would produce.

### Test 2: `test_index_regen_partial_index_participation_change`
Create a partial index with `WHERE active = 1`. Rebase an UPDATE that sets `active = 0`.
Verify an `IndexDelete` is emitted (row exits the partial index) and no `IndexInsert`
is emitted.

### Test 3: `test_index_regen_partial_index_entry_to_entry`
Partial index with `WHERE score > 50`. Rebase an UPDATE that changes `score` from 60 to 70.
Verify `IndexDelete(old_key)` + `IndexInsert(new_key)` are emitted since participation
stays true but key changes.

### Test 4: `test_index_regen_unique_constraint_violation_aborts`
UNIQUE index on column `email`. Rebase an UPDATE that changes email to a value that
already exists (for a different rowid) in the new committed base. Verify rebase aborts
with `SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_UNIQUE` and does NOT silently merge.

### Test 5: `test_index_regen_expression_index`
Expression index on `lower(name)`. Rebase an UPDATE that changes `name` from 'Alice'
to 'Bob'. Verify old key `lower('Alice')` is deleted and new key `lower('Bob')` is
inserted, with correct affinity and encoding.

### Test 6: `test_index_regen_no_op_when_key_unchanged`
Rebase an UPDATE that changes a non-indexed column. Verify no index ops are emitted
for indexes whose keys and partial predicates are independent of the changed columns.

### Test 7: `test_index_regen_multiple_indexes_same_table`
Table with 3 indexes (ordinary, unique, partial). Rebase an UPDATE that affects all
three. Verify correct ops are emitted for each index independently, in the correct order.

### Test 8: `test_index_regen_overflow_key_handling`
Index key that exceeds the B-tree cell payload threshold. Verify overflow pages are
correctly freed on `IndexDelete` and correctly allocated on `IndexInsert` during rebase.

### Commits
0cba28b — `feat(fsqlite-pager): rollback journal format + lock-byte page (bd-188d)` (initial index_regen.rs)

### Implementation Summary

**index_regen.rs (~1700 lines):**

**Core Types:**
- `Collation`: Binary/Nocase/Rtrim collation ordering
- `IndexKeyPart`: Column{col_idx, affinity, collation} | Expression{expr, affinity, collation}
- `IndexDef`: Full secondary index schema (index_id, table_id, unique, key_parts, where_predicate, table_column_affinities)
- `IndexRegenError`: UniqueConstraintViolation | MalformedRecord | ColumnOutOfBounds | ExprEvalError
- `IndexRegenOps`: Vec of IntentOpKind ops to emit in rebased intent log
- `UniqueChecker` trait: check_unique(index_id, key_bytes, exclude_rowid) → Option<RowId>
- `NoOpUniqueChecker`: test/non-UNIQUE stub

**7-Step Algorithm (regenerate_index_ops):**
1. `discard_stale_index_ops()`: Filters stale IndexDelete/IndexInsert ops for rebased (table, rowid)
2. Enumerate secondary indexes from provided schema
3. `row_participates()`: Evaluates partial index WHERE predicate for base and updated rows
4. `compute_index_key()`: Builds key bytes via affinity coercion + collation normalization + serialize_record
5. Emit ops based on participation delta: base-only→Delete, updated-only→Insert, both+key-change→Delete+Insert, both+same-key→no-op
6. UNIQUE enforcement via `UniqueChecker` trait; violations return `IndexRegenError::UniqueConstraintViolation`
7. Overflow handling delegated to B-tree layer (key bytes computed at full length)

**Expression Evaluation (eval_rebase_expr):**
- Full recursive evaluator for RebaseExpr: ColumnRef, Literal, UnaryOp, BinaryOp, FunctionCall, Cast, Case, Coalesce, NullIf, Concat
- Arithmetic: Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide (div-by-zero→NULL), Remainder, bitwise ops, shifts
- Functions: abs, lower, upper, length, typeof, ifnull, max, min
- SQLite semantics: NULL propagation, exact float comparison, truthiness rules

### Tests (19 total, all passing)
1. test_index_regen_ordinary_index_key_change — Delete+Insert on key change
2. test_index_regen_partial_index_participation_change — Insert when entering partial index
3. test_index_regen_partial_index_entry_to_entry — No-op when key unchanged in partial index
4. test_index_regen_unique_constraint_violation_aborts — UNIQUE violation aborts with correct error
5. test_index_regen_expression_index — lower() expression index produces correct keys
6. test_index_regen_no_op_when_key_unchanged — No ops when non-indexed column updated
7. test_index_regen_multiple_indexes_same_table — 4 ops for 2 indexes both with key changes
8. test_index_regen_overflow_key_handling — 4000-byte keys encoded correctly
9. test_discard_stale_index_ops — Correctly filters stale ops by (table, rowid)
10. test_column_out_of_bounds_error — Schema mismatch error
11. test_nocase_collation_key_normalization — NOCASE normalizes "HELLO"/"hello" to same key
12. test_eval_rebase_expr_arithmetic — Add/Multiply on column refs
13. test_eval_division_by_zero — Division by zero returns NULL
14. test_index_regen_partial_leaves_participation — IndexDelete when leaving partial index
15. test_index_regen_unique_no_conflict — UNIQUE index with NoOpUniqueChecker passes
16. test_apply_column_updates — Correct column update application with affinity
17. test_malformed_record_error — Bad record bytes return MalformedRecord error
18. test_composite_index_key — Multi-column composite key with partial update
19. test_unique_checker_with_btreemap — Realistic BTreeMap-backed unique checker

### Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Stale index ops correctly discarded for rebased rows
- [x] Partial index participation evaluated correctly for both base and updated rows
- [x] UNIQUE constraint violations abort with UniqueConstraintViolation error
- [x] Expression indexes produce correct keys matching VDBE encoding
- [x] Overflow page chain key bytes computed at full length (B-tree layer delegation)
- [x] Generated index ops match what a fresh encode would produce
- [x] All types re-exported from fsqlite-mvcc crate root
- [x] Clippy pedantic+nursery clean

### Spec Content (Lines 9168-9195)
First-committer-wins: When T2 attempts to commit and discovers T1 already committed modifications to the same page(s), T2 MUST detect this via CommitIndex lookup. The response depends on whether deterministic rebase (§5.10) can resolve the conflict.

Conflict detection pipeline:
1. Lock acquisition: T2 acquires exclusive page locks for its write_set
2. CommitIndex check: For each page in write_set, if CommitIndex[pgno] > T2.begin_seq → conflict
3. Rebase attempt: If conflict pages overlap, attempt deterministic rebase (§5.10)
4. SSI validation: Even if rebase succeeds, still run SSI check (§5.7.3)
5. Resolution: Commit on success, SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT on failure

2. test_no_conflict_different_pages: T1 and T2 touch different pages → both commit
3. test_conflict_with_successful_rebase: Same page but commuting ops → both commit
4. test_conflict_response_sqlite_busy: Non-rebasable conflict → SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT
5. test_commit_index_lookup_correctness: CommitIndex correctly tracks latest commit per page

PURPOSE: Implement page lock table, FCW commit validation, and serialized/concurrent mode interaction.

## Page Lock Table Implementation (normative)

### Concurrent Mode (cross-process)
- SharedPageLockTable in foo.db.fsqlite-shm (§5.6.3) is THE canonical lock table
- ALL page-level writer exclusion MUST be enforced via shared-memory table, NOT in-process HashMap

### Normal commit/abort (fast path)
- Release page locks by iterating in-process page_locks set (touch only locked pages)

### Crash cleanup (slow path)
- MUST use shared-memory scan release_page_locks_for(txn_id) (§5.6.3) -- crashed process has no in-process set

## Single-Process Reference Implementation (NOT cross-process safe)
- 64-shard InProcessPageLockTable (parking_lot::Mutex<HashMap<PageNumber, TxnId>>)
- Shard selection: pgno.get() as usize & (LOCK_TABLE_SHARDS - 1)
- try_acquire: vacant→insert, occupied→check same txn (idempotent) or SQLITE_BUSY
- release: remove entry, panic if not held by txn
- release_all: iterate per-txn lock set (O(W) where W = write set size)
  - Production MAY group by shard to reduce lock acquisitions

## Commit Validation Algorithm (First-Committer-Wins)
- validate_commit(T, commit_index):
  - For each page in write_set: if commit_index.latest_commit_seq(pgno) > T.snapshot.high → conflict
  - On conflict: attempt algebraic merge (§5.10)
    - If merge possible: perform_merge
    - If not: return SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT (retryable)

## Serialized ↔ Concurrent Mode Interaction

### While Serialized-mode writer is Active (holding global write mutex):
- Concurrent txns MAY BEGIN and read normally
- Any Concurrent-mode page write lock attempt MUST fail with SQLITE_BUSY
  (allowing concurrent writers would violate SQLite single-writer contract)

### While any Concurrent-mode writer is Active (holds any page locks):
- Acquiring Serialized writer exclusion (BEGIN IMMEDIATE/EXCLUSIVE/DEFERRED upgrade) MUST fail with SQLITE_BUSY
- DEFERRED read-only begins remain permitted; only writer upgrade excluded

### Cross-Process Implementation
- SharedMemoryLayout maintains serialized_writer indicator (token + lease)
- Set when Serialized txn acquires writer exclusion, cleared at commit/abort
- Concurrent-mode write paths MUST check this indicator before acquiring page locks

### check_serialized_writer_exclusion(shm) Algorithm
- Load token (Acquire); if 0 → Ok
- Check expiry + process_alive(pid, birth)
- If alive and valid lease → SQLITE_BUSY
- If stale (lease expired or owner dead): CAS clear + retry loop
  - CAS failure means token changed (someone else cleared or new writer installed)
  - MUST retry to avoid returning Ok while new serialized writer is active

### Serialized Writer Acquisition Ordering (5 steps)
1. Acquire global serialized writer exclusion
2. Publish shared indicator (serialized_writer_token != 0, Release ordering)
3. Drain concurrent writers: wait until no outstanding page locks from Concurrent-mode txns
4. Perform writes
5. On commit/abort: CAS clear indicator + release global exclusion

### External Interop Hook (Compatibility mode)
- Concurrent-mode exclusion meaningless if legacy writer bypasses .fsqlite-shm
- Compatibility mode with .fsqlite-shm MUST exclude legacy writers (§5.6.6.1, §5.6.7)
- FORBIDDEN: multi-writer MVCC while legacy writers permitted

PARENT EPIC: bd-3t3
DEPENDS ON: bd-3t3.8 (SharedPageLockTable), bd-3t3.3 (Transaction Lifecycle), bd-3t3.1 (Core Types)

Dependencies:
  -> bd-3t3 (parent-child) - §5: MVCC Formal Model (Revised)
  -> bd-3t3.8 (blocks) - §5.6.3 SharedPageLockTable: Cross-Process Exclusive Locks
  -> bd-3t3.3 (blocks) - §5.4 Transaction Lifecycle (Begin/Read/Write/Commit/Abort)
  -> bd-3t3.1 (blocks) - §5.1 MVCC Core Types

Dependents:
  <- bd-1h3b (blocks) - §5.10.2-5.10.4 Deterministic Rebase + Physical Merge + Merge Policy
  <- bd-1onb (blocks) - §5.9.1-5.9.2 Write Coordinator Sequencers (Native + WAL Paths)
